title: Prophet
description: In Islamic tradition, the Prophet Muhammad (c. 570 – 632 CE) is revered as the last prophet sent by God (Allah) to humanity. Born in Mecca, he received his first revelation around the age of 40 in the cave of Hira, where the angel Gabriel (Jibril) commanded him to recite verses that would later form the Quran, the holy book of Islam. Muhammad's teachings emphasized the oneness of God (Tawhid), the importance of moral and ethical conduct, and the establishment of a just society. His message initially faced resistance in Mecca, leading to the Hijra (migration) to Medina in 622 CE, a pivotal event marking the beginning of the Islamic calendar. In Medina, Muhammad established a community based on Islamic principles, serving as both a religious leader and a political figure. After several battles and treaties, Muhammad returned to Mecca in 630 CE, peacefully conquering the city and cleansing the Kaaba of idols. He unified most of Arabia under Islam before his death in 632 CE. Muhammad's life and teachings, known as the Sunnah, serve as a model for Muslims worldwide, guiding their beliefs, practices, and daily lives.
divine_attributes:
Birth
570 CE, Makkah (Year of the Elephant)


Death
632 CE (aged 63), Madinah


Status
Final Prophet, Seal of Prophethood


Known As
Al-Amin (The Trustworthy), Al-Sadiq (The Truthful)


Family
Father: Abdullah, Mother: Aminah, Wife: Khadijah (first)


Children
Fatimah (daughter), and others


Tribe
Quraysh (Banu Hashim clan)


Revelation Period
610-632 CE (23 years)


Early Life
Birth and Orphanhood

                Muhammad was born in 570 CE during the "Year of the Elephant," when Abraha's army attempted to
                destroy the Kaaba
name: Prophet
summary: Also known as 🕌 Prophet Muhammad. Associated with unknown.

worshipPractices.name: Salat (Prayer)
worshipPractices.description: Muslims perform five daily prayers facing the Kaaba in Mecca. These prayers involve specific rituals, recitations from the Quran, and physical postures, serving as a direct connection and submission to Allah.

worshipPractices.name: Sawm (Fasting)
worshipPractices.description: During the month of Ramadan, Muslims abstain from eating, drinking, and other physical needs from dawn until sunset. This practice cultivates self-discipline, empathy for the less fortunate, and spiritual reflection.

worshipPractices.name: Zakat (Charity)
worshipPractices.description: Giving a portion of one's wealth to the poor and needy is a mandatory act of charity in Islam. It purifies wealth, fosters social responsibility, and helps to alleviate poverty within the community.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr
festivals.timing: Celebrated at the end of Ramadan
festivals.description: Marks the end of the month-long fast of Ramadan. It is a time for feasting, celebration, and giving thanks to Allah. Muslims attend special prayers, visit family and friends, and exchange gifts.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha
festivals.timing: Celebrated during the month of Dhul Hijjah
festivals.description: Commemorates the willingness of Ibrahim (Abraham) to sacrifice his son Ismail (Ishmael) as an act of obedience to Allah. Muslims sacrifice an animal (usually a sheep or goat) and share the meat with family, friends, and the poor.

festivals.name: Mawlid an-Nabi
festivals.timing: Celebrated in Rabi' al-awwal, the third month of the Islamic calendar
festivals.description: Commemorates the birth of the Prophet Muhammad. Observed with religious lectures, poetry recitations, and acts of charity, emphasizing his teachings and exemplary character.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Prophet Muhammad's teachings and example continue to inspire billions of Muslims worldwide, influencing their moral compass, social interactions, and legal frameworks. His life story and sayings (Hadith) are foundational texts for Islamic scholarship and provide guidance on various aspects of life, impacting art, literature, and ethical discussions in modern society.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Hermes acts as a messenger between the gods and humans, similar to a prophet conveying divine messages. Both are seen as intermediaries.
Odin sought wisdom and knowledge through sacrifice and journeys, gaining prophetic abilities and insights. This mirrors the role of a prophet receiving divine revelation through spiritual dedication.
Thoth is the god of wisdom, writing, and knowledge, often depicted as a scribe recording divine decrees. This parallels a prophet's role in communicating and preserving sacred teachings.
Rishis are seers and sages who receive divine revelations and transmit them to humanity. They are revered for their wisdom and spiritual insight, similar to the respect given to prophets.

associations.name: Star
associations.significance: A guiding light, symbolizing divine guidance and revelation.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Symbolizes paradise, growth, and the flourishing of faith.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Represents the establishment of fairness and equity in society.

associations.name: Compassion
associations.significance: Highlights the Prophet's mercy and empathy towards all beings.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Represents the concept of monotheism and the unity of God.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Signifies purity, cleansing, and the source of life and knowledge.

associations.name: Date Palm
associations.significance: Represents sustenance, resilience, and the blessings of the land.

companions.name: Angel Gabriel (Jibril)
companions.description: The archangel who delivered divine revelations to Prophet Muhammad, serving as a crucial link between God and the Prophet. He guided and supported the Prophet throughout his mission.

companions.name: Abu Bakr
companions.description: One of the closest companions and the first caliph of Islam. He was known for his unwavering loyalty and support of the Prophet.

companions.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
companions.description: The cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad, revered for his wisdom and bravery. He was a key figure in the early Islamic community.

companions.name: Khadija bint Khuwaylid
companions.description: The first wife of Prophet Muhammad and a strong supporter of his mission. She provided him with emotional and financial support.

companions.name: Buraq
companions.description: A mythical steed that transported Prophet Muhammad during the Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj). It carried him from Mecca to Jerusalem and then through the heavens.

extendedContent.title: Prophethood and Revelation
extendedContent.content:
In Islamic theology, prophethood (Nubuwwa) is a central concept. Prophets are chosen by Allah to deliver His message to humanity. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) is considered the last prophet in this lineage, the 'Seal of the Prophets' (Khatam an-Nabiyyin). His prophethood began around the age of forty in the year 610 CE, when he received his first revelation in the cave of Hira near Mecca. This revelation, according to Islamic belief, was delivered by the angel Gabriel (Jibril). The content of these revelations forms the Quran, which Muslims believe to be the literal word of God.

The initial revelations were short, poetic verses that emphasized the oneness of God (Tawhid) and the importance of moral conduct. As the revelations continued over the next 23 years, they addressed various aspects of life, including worship, law, ethics, and social justice. The Quran itself recounts stories of previous prophets, such as Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus, emphasizing the continuity of the divine message throughout history. These prophets are seen as messengers of Allah who faced similar challenges in conveying His message to their respective communities.

The process of revelation (Wahy) is described in Islamic tradition as a profound and transformative experience for the Prophet Muhammad. The Quran mentions various ways in which revelation occurred, including direct communication, dreams, and the appearance of the angel Gabriel. These experiences are considered a testament to the divine origin of the Quran and the unique role of the Prophet Muhammad as Allah's messenger to mankind.

extendedContent.title: The Hijra and Establishment of the Muslim Community
extendedContent.content:
The Hijra, or migration, of the Prophet Muhammad and his followers from Mecca to Medina in 622 CE marks a pivotal moment in Islamic history. Faced with increasing persecution and opposition in Mecca, the Prophet accepted an invitation from the people of Medina (then known as Yathrib) to mediate their disputes and establish a community based on Islamic principles. This migration was not merely a physical relocation but a fundamental shift in the trajectory of Islam, transforming it from a persecuted minority to a growing and organized community.

In Medina, the Prophet Muhammad established the first Muslim state, based on the principles of justice, equality, and consultation. He drafted the Constitution of Medina, a groundbreaking document that outlined the rights and responsibilities of all citizens, regardless of their religious affiliation. This document established a framework for peaceful coexistence between Muslims, Jews, and other religious groups in Medina. The Prophet Muhammad also played a key role in resolving disputes, leading prayers, and guiding the community in all aspects of life.

The Hijra is commemorated as the beginning of the Islamic calendar (Hijri calendar) and symbolizes the triumph of faith over adversity. It represents the willingness of the Prophet and his followers to sacrifice everything for their beliefs and to establish a society based on divine guidance. The events surrounding the Hijra continue to inspire Muslims around the world and serve as a reminder of the importance of perseverance, resilience, and faith in the face of challenges.

extendedContent.title: Miracles and Signs of Prophethood
extendedContent.content:
In Islamic tradition, prophets are often associated with miracles (Mu'jizat) that serve as signs of their divine mission and confirmation of their prophethood. While the Quran itself is considered the ultimate miracle (I'jaz al-Quran) of the Prophet Muhammad, signifying its unmatched literary and spiritual excellence, numerous other events are attributed to him as signs of his prophethood. These miracles are not seen as violations of natural laws but rather as extraordinary events that occur with Allah's permission to demonstrate His power and support for His messengers.

One of the most well-known miracles associated with the Prophet Muhammad is the Isra and Mi'raj, the Night Journey and Ascension. According to Islamic tradition, the Prophet Muhammad was miraculously transported from Mecca to Jerusalem in a single night (Isra) and then ascended to the heavens (Mi'raj), where he met with previous prophets and received instructions from Allah. This event is mentioned in the Quran (Surah Al-Isra, 17:1) and is considered a profound spiritual experience that demonstrates the Prophet's unique connection to the divine.

Other miracles attributed to the Prophet Muhammad include the splitting of the moon (Shaqq al-Qamar), the multiplication of food, the healing of the sick, and the ability to communicate with animals. While the interpretation and authenticity of these miracles may vary among different Islamic scholars and traditions, they collectively serve to reinforce the belief in the Prophet's divine mission and his unique status as Allah's messenger.

extendedContent.title: The Prophet as Exemplar: Sunnah and Hadith
extendedContent.content:
Beyond the Quran, the Sunnah, or the Prophet Muhammad's example, serves as a crucial source of guidance for Muslims. The Sunnah encompasses his words, actions, and tacit approvals, providing a practical framework for living a life in accordance with Islamic principles. The Hadith are the recorded narrations of the Prophet's words and deeds, meticulously preserved and transmitted through generations of scholars. These Hadith, along with the Quran, form the basis of Islamic law (Sharia) and provide guidance on a wide range of issues, from prayer and fasting to marriage and commerce.

The Prophet Muhammad is considered the perfect exemplar (Uswa Hasana) for Muslims, embodying the virtues of compassion, justice, honesty, and wisdom. His life serves as a model for how to interact with others, treat family members, conduct business, and worship Allah. Muslims strive to emulate the Prophet's character and actions in all aspects of their lives, seeking to follow his example in their personal conduct, social interactions, and spiritual practices.

The study of the Sunnah and Hadith is a complex and rigorous discipline, requiring careful analysis of the authenticity and reliability of the narrations. Islamic scholars have developed elaborate methodologies for evaluating Hadith, ensuring that only the most credible and authentic narrations are accepted as valid sources of guidance. The Sunnah and Hadith provide a rich and nuanced understanding of Islamic teachings and offer a practical roadmap for Muslims seeking to live a life pleasing to Allah.

shortDescription: The last prophet in Islam, receiving divine revelations and unifying Arabia under a new faith.
